title: When walking the same route changes how you see a city
summary: Reflect on how walking the same daily route over months or years slowly changes what you notice about a city, and what that reveals about attention.
topic: How walking the same route repeatedly changes how you see a city
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about a route you have walked or cycled repeatedly in a city, and how repetition changed what you noticed. Use the present perfect for accumulated observation, the past simple for specific examples, a relative clause, and 'however' or 'although'.
hintStarters: I have walked the same fifteen minutes between my flat and the language school in Edinburgh almost every weekday for two years, and the route has changed more in my eyes than it has on the map…
If someone had told me at the beginning that I would still find new details on the same stretch of pavement after eighteen months, I would have…
Although the obvious explanation for noticing more things is having more free attention, what I have actually experienced is something stranger, which is that…

hintVocab.phrase: to take the same way home
to notice something for the first time
hintVocab.meaningVi: nhận ra điều gì đó lần đầu tiên
nhịp điệu của một con phố

hintVocab.phrase: to feel grounded in a place
hintVocab.meaningVi: cảm thấy gắn bó với một nơi
một người đi bộ chú ý

sampleText:
I have walked the same fifteen minutes between my flat and the language school in Edinburgh almost every weekday for two years, and the route has changed more in my eyes than it has on the map. When I started, I noticed the obvious things: the long curve of Buccleuch Street, the bookshop on the corner of South Bridge, the wide stone steps of an old church. Within a month I could have drawn a reasonable sketch of the route. Yet two years later, I am still finding details I have somehow walked past five hundred times without seeing — a date carved above a doorway, a small herb garden two floors above the pavement, a metal hook that must once have held a sign that no longer exists.

What I have come to understand is that walking a route repeatedly does not exhaust it; it slowly rebuilds it. In the first weeks the mind is busy navigating, so it sees only landmarks. After a few months the navigation becomes automatic and the attention has nothing to do, so it begins to wander upwards, sideways, into the corners. The route stops being a path between two points and becomes a kind of long, narrow neighbourhood that you happen to know better than the people who only see it from a bus window. Although tourists who pass through this stretch of Old Town in an afternoon may take more photographs than I have in two years, they cannot tell you which shop changed hands in February or which old man waits at the bus stop with the same green umbrella whenever it rains.

What repetition really teaches, I think, is that attention is not a fixed quantity you bring to a place; it is something that opens up in response to time. The same fifteen minutes contain more or less depending on how long you have been giving them to the same patch of pavement. However, this only happens if you walk. A bus or a car compresses the route into a blur, and even a bicycle moves too fast for the second-floor windows to come into focus. I have come to think of my route as a slow form of friendship with a piece of the city — not a friendship made through anything dramatic, but through the simple fact of returning at the same hour, in the same direction, until the place finally begins to show you the things it does not show strangers. Walking a route every day for two years is one of the very few ways a foreigner can become, quietly, a local of a single street.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: I could have drawn a reasonable sketch of the route
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Tôi đã có thể vẽ một bản phác thảo hợp lý về tuyến đường

sampleAnnotations.phrase: I am still finding details I have somehow walked past five hundred times without seeing
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Tôi vẫn đang tìm thấy những chi tiết mà bằng cách nào đó tôi đã đi ngang qua năm trăm lần mà không thấy

sampleAnnotations.phrase: a metal hook that must once have held a sign that no longer exists

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the navigation becomes automatic and the attention has nothing to do

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for accumulated experience and observation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I have walked the same fifteen minutes… for two years' và 'I have come to understand' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để mô tả một hoạt động hoặc nhận thức tích lũy từ quá khứ đến hiện tại. Với khoảng thời gian dùng 'for + period' hoặc 'since + point in time'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I have walked the same fifteen minutes… for two years' and 'I have come to understand' use the present perfect for an activity or realisation accumulated from the past to the present. Use 'for + period' or 'since + point in time' for the duration.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal of past deduction: 'must once have held'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'a metal hook that must once have held a sign that no longer exists' dùng 'must have + past participle' với 'once' để suy đoán chắc chắn về quá khứ xa. Cấu trúc này rất tự nhiên trong văn miêu tả thành phố cổ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'a metal hook that must once have held a sign that no longer exists' uses 'must have + past participle' with 'once' for confident deduction about the distant past. This structure is very natural when describing old cities.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tôi đã đi bộ cùng mười lăm phút giữa căn hộ của mình và trường ngôn ngữ ở Edinburgh gần như mỗi ngày trong tuần suốt hai năm, và tuyến đường đã thay đổi nhiều hơn trong mắt tôi so với trên bản đồ. Khi tôi bắt đầu, tôi để ý những điều hiển nhiên: đường cong dài của phố Buccleuch, hiệu sách ở góc South Bridge, các bậc đá rộng của một nhà thờ cổ. Trong vòng một tháng, tôi đã có thể vẽ một bản phác thảo hợp lý về tuyến đường. Vậy mà hai năm sau, tôi vẫn đang tìm thấy những chi tiết mà bằng cách nào đó tôi đã đi ngang qua năm trăm lần mà không thấy — một ngày tháng được khắc phía trên một khung cửa, một khu vườn rau thơm nhỏ trên hai tầng so với vỉa hè, một cái móc kim loại mà hẳn đã từng treo một tấm biển không còn tồn tại.

Điều tôi đã dần hiểu là đi cùng một tuyến đường lặp đi lặp lại không làm cạn kiệt nó; nó dần xây dựng lại nó. Trong những tuần đầu, tâm trí bận rộn với việc định hướng, nên nó chỉ thấy những điểm mốc. Sau vài tháng, việc định hướng trở nên tự động và sự chú ý không có việc gì để làm, nên nó bắt đầu lang thang lên trên, sang bên, vào các góc. Tuyến đường ngừng là con đường giữa hai điểm và trở thành một kiểu khu phố dài và hẹp mà bạn tình cờ biết rõ hơn những người chỉ nhìn thấy nó từ cửa sổ xe buýt. Mặc dù khách du lịch đi qua đoạn này của Old Town trong một buổi chiều có thể chụp nhiều ảnh hơn tôi đã chụp trong hai năm, họ không thể cho bạn biết cửa hàng nào đã đổi chủ vào tháng Hai hay ông lão nào chờ ở trạm xe buýt với cùng cây dù xanh mỗi khi trời mưa.

Điều mà sự lặp lại thực sự dạy, tôi nghĩ, là sự chú ý không phải là một lượng cố định bạn mang đến một nơi; nó là một điều mở ra để đáp lại thời gian. Cùng mười lăm phút chứa nhiều hay ít tùy thuộc vào việc bạn đã dành chúng cho cùng mảng vỉa hè đó bao lâu. Tuy nhiên, điều này chỉ xảy ra nếu bạn đi bộ. Một chiếc xe buýt hay xe hơi nén tuyến đường thành một vệt mờ, và ngay cả một chiếc xe đạp cũng di chuyển quá nhanh để các cửa sổ tầng hai có thể vào nét. Tôi đã dần nghĩ về tuyến đường của mình như một dạng tình bạn chậm rãi với một phần của thành phố — không phải tình bạn được tạo qua bất cứ điều gì kịch tính, mà qua sự thật đơn giản là quay trở lại cùng giờ, theo cùng hướng, cho đến khi nơi đó cuối cùng bắt đầu cho bạn thấy những điều nó không cho người lạ thấy. Đi cùng một tuyến đường mỗi ngày trong hai năm là một trong rất ít cách mà một người nước ngoài có thể trở thành, lặng lẽ, một người địa phương của một con phố duy nhất.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly with 'for + period'?
options: I have walked the same fifteen minutes for two years.
I am walking the same fifteen minutes for two years.
I have walking the same fifteen minutes for two years.
I walked the same fifteen minutes for two years now.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'have walked' with 'for + period' expresses an ongoing repeated action from the past to now.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect + 'for + duration' for ongoing repeated actions.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'must have + past participle' correctly?
options: A metal hook that must once have held a sign that no longer exists.
A metal hook that must once held a sign that no longer exists.
A metal hook that must once has held a sign that no longer exists.
A metal hook that must once have holding a sign that no longer exists.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Must have + past participle' is the past deduction structure. Past participle of 'hold' is 'held'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Must have + past participle'; past participle of 'hold' is 'held'.

options: Although tourists may take more photographs than I have in two years, they cannot tell you which shop changed hands.
Although tourists may take more photographs than I have in two years, but they cannot tell you which shop changed hands.
Although, tourists may take more photographs than I have in two years they cannot tell you which shop changed hands.
Tourists may take more photographs than I have in two years, although however they cannot tell you which shop changed hands.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses a relative clause?
options: Tourists who pass through this stretch of Old Town in an afternoon.
Tourists which pass through this stretch of Old Town in an afternoon.
Tourists they pass through this stretch of Old Town in an afternoon.
Tourists who they pass through this stretch of Old Town in an afternoon.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Who' is used for people in relative clauses; 'which' is for things; no repeated subject pronoun.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'who' for people; no repeated subject pronoun.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'come to think of' correctly?
options: I have come to think of my route as a slow form of friendship.
I have came to think of my route as a slow form of friendship.
I have come thinking of my route as a slow form of friendship.
I came to think of my route as a slow form of friendship now.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'have come to + base verb' for gradual realisation; 'think of' is the correct collocation.
mcQuestions.hint: Past participle of 'come' is 'come'; 'have come to + base verb'.

options: The old man waits at the bus stop with the same green umbrella whenever it rains.
The old man waits at the bus stop with the same green umbrella whenever it will rain.
The old man waits at the bus stop with the same green umbrella whenever does it rain.
The old man waits at the bus stop with the same green umbrella whenever it rains then.
mcQuestions.explanation: Time clauses with 'whenever' use the present simple even for future reference; standard subject-verb order; no extra 'then'.
mcQuestions.hint: Use present simple in time clauses; no 'will' after 'whenever'.

options: The same fifteen minutes contain more or less depending on how long you have been giving them to the same patch of pavement.
The same fifteen minutes contain more or less depending of how long you have been giving them to the same patch of pavement.
The same fifteen minutes contain more or less depend on how long you have been giving them to the same patch of pavement.
The same fifteen minutes contain more or less depending to how long you have been giving them to the same patch of pavement.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Depending on + noun/clause' is the correct collocation; '-ing' form, not the bare verb 'depend'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'happen to + base verb'?
options: A long, narrow neighbourhood that you happen to know better than the people who only see it from a bus window.
A long, narrow neighbourhood that you happen know better than the people who only see it from a bus window.
A long, narrow neighbourhood that you happen knowing better than the people who only see it from a bus window.
A long, narrow neighbourhood that you happen to knowing better than the people who only see it from a bus window.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Happen to + base verb' means 'by chance do/be'. 'To' is required; the base verb follows.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Happen to + base verb'; 'to' is required, base verb after.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'too fast + for + object + to + base verb' correctly?
options: Even a bicycle moves too fast for the second-floor windows to come into focus.
Even a bicycle moves too fast for the second-floor windows for coming into focus.
Even a bicycle moves too much fast for the second-floor windows to come into focus.
Even a bicycle moves too fast that the second-floor windows to come into focus.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Too + adjective/adverb + for + object + to + base verb' is the fixed structure.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Too + adj + for + object + to + base verb'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'in response to' correctly?
options: Attention is something that opens up in response to time.
Attention is something that opens up in response of time.
Attention is something that opens up in response with time.
Attention is something that opens up at response to time.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'In response to + noun' is the correct fixed phrase; 'of', 'with', and 'at' are all wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: 'In response to + noun' is the standard fixed phrase.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'one of the very few ways' correctly?
options: Walking a route every day for two years is one of the very few ways a foreigner can become a local of a single street.
Walking a route every day for two years is one of the very few way a foreigner can become a local of a single street.
Walking a route every day for two years is one of very few ways a foreigner can become a local of a single street.
Walking a route every day for two years is one of the very few ways a foreigner can to become a local of a single street.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'One of the very few ways' takes the plural noun 'ways'; the article 'the' is required; modal + bare infinitive ('can become').
mcQuestions.hint: 'One of the + plural noun'; modal + bare infinitive.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If real attention only opens up over months of repetition, what does that suggest about how we travel — and whether short visits to many cities give us less than long visits to a few?
